task: Log-likelihood keyword extraction (node vs. reference corpus, explicit LL threshold and minimum frequency cut-off)
notable human research: Dunning, T. (1993). Accurate methods for the statistics of surprise and coincidence. Computational Linguistics, 19(1), 61–74.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: A corpus linguist can readily execute LL extraction using standard tools like AntConc or WordSmith given explicit thresholds.
Research Significance — Explanation: LL keyword extraction is foundational to corpus comparison studies and underpins a large share of corpus-based findings.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can fully automate LL computation given tokenised corpora and explicit thresholds with no human intervention needed.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Fully automatable end-to-end within a decade as corpus pipelines mature further.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task is mathematically closed-form with fully specifiable inputs and outputs, leaving no theoretical barrier to full automation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The LL formula, window parameters, and frequency cut-offs are explicitly documented in Dunning (1993) and tool manuals.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Threshold, frequency minimum, and corpus pair are typically reported explicitly in publications using this method.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Given identical corpora and parameters, LL scores are deterministic floating-point computations with no stochastic element.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Libraries such as NLTK, AntConc scripts, and corpus-comparison packages implement LL extraction out of the box.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No qualitative judgement is required beyond choosing parameters; the computation is entirely arithmetic.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication success can be verified by exact numerical match of top-N keyword lists and LL scores against published tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Parameters such as span, frequency floor, and corpus size can be systematically varied to map keyness sensitivity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: An AI output requires only automated comparison of ranked keyword lists against published figures, needing minimal human audit time.

task: BIC-based keyword ranking (BIC as association measure)
notable human research: Wilson, A. (2013). Embracing Bayes factors for key item analysis in corpus linguistics. In M. Bieswanger & A. Koll-Stobbe (Eds.), New approaches to the study of linguistic variability. Peter Lang.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: BIC-based ranking is straightforward for a trained corpus linguist using standard statistical software.
Research Significance — Explanation: BIC as an association measure offers a statistically principled alternative and is increasingly cited in keyword analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: BIC computation is a closed-form formula that current AI can execute automatically once corpus frequencies are obtained.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is inevitable as Bayesian corpus metrics become standard pipeline components.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: BIC ranking is fully deterministic given corpus frequencies, so theoretical maximum replaceability is complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Wilson (2013) details the BIC formula and its parameterisation clearly enough for automated parsing.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Corpus sizes and frequency counts needed for BIC are almost always reported in the methods section.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: BIC is a deterministic function of observed and expected frequencies, producing identical results on identical inputs.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R packages such as quanteda and Python corpus tools can compute BIC scores with minor custom scripting.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: The task requires no interpretive judgement; it is a direct mathematical ranking of frequency ratios.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: BIC keyword lists can be numerically verified against published ranked lists from the same corpus.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Sensitivity to frequency thresholds and corpus size can be systematically explored by varying those parameters.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification reduces to automated list comparison, requiring negligible human audit time.

task: Multi-word keyword / n-gram keyness (bigrams or trigrams)
notable human research: O'Donnell, M. B. (2011). The adjusted frequency list: A method to produce cluster-sensitive frequency lists. ICAME Journal, 35, 135–169.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: N-gram keyness extraction is feasible for corpus linguists using tools like AntConc or SketchEngine's n-gram modules.
Research Significance — Explanation: Multi-word keyness extends single-word approaches to phrasal patterns, adding important nuance to keyword analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can enumerate bigram/trigram frequencies and apply keyness statistics fully automatically.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead improvements in parsing and n-gram indexing will make this entirely routine.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task is a direct extension of unigram keyness to higher-order n-grams with no theoretical barrier to automation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: N-gram extraction parameters (n, frequency floor, association measure) are specified in the cited literature.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Minimum frequency thresholds and n values are typically stated explicitly in papers using this method.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: N-gram frequency counts are deterministic given a fixed tokenisation and corpus; keyness scores follow deterministically.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: SketchEngine, AntConc, and corpus Python libraries all support n-gram frequency and keyness computation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive load is needed; the task is purely enumerative with a fixed statistical criterion.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication can be checked by matching top-N n-gram keyword lists and scores against published output.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying n, frequency floor, or reference corpus size provides systematic sensitivity mapping.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated list diffing against published tables allows rapid verification with minimal human involvement.

task: Semantic category keyword clustering (e.g., post-hoc USAS tagging)
notable human research: Rayson, P. (2008). From key words to key semantic domains. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 519–549.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Clustering keywords by USAS semantic category requires running a tagger and grouping outputs, feasible but tool-dependent.
Research Significance — Explanation: Semantic clustering moves beyond surface keyness to thematic patterning, adding interpretive value to keyword analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run USAS tagging and group keywords semi-automatically, though tag disambiguation reduces perfect replaceability.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved neural semantic taggers over the next decade will raise automation to near-full for tagging and grouping.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because domain-specific USAS tag ambiguity may always require some human validation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Rayson (2008) describes the USAS tagging pipeline, but tag ambiguity resolution is only partially specified.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: The USAS tagset is published, but which tag to apply to ambiguous lexical items is often left to tagger defaults.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: USAS tagging introduces probabilistic disambiguation, meaning identical inputs can yield slightly different clusterings.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: The USAS CLAWS tagger is a mature tool, but integration with custom keyness workflows requires bespoke scripting.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed to interpret and label merged USAS semantic categories meaningfully.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Cluster memberships can be compared to published category lists, though category boundary decisions introduce minor uncertainty.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Robustness to tagger settings and tag merging schemes can be explored by varying USAS parameters systematically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human review of tag assignments for boundary cases adds moderate audit overhead compared to purely arithmetic tasks.

task: Diachronic keyword comparison (keyness across two time periods)
notable human research: Baker, P. (2011). Times may change, but we will always have money: Diachronic variation in recent British English. Journal of English Linguistics, 39(1), 65–88.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Diachronic keyword comparison is straightforward for researchers with access to period-divided corpora and keyness tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Tracking lexical change across time periods is central to historical and sociolinguistic corpus studies.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can split corpora by period and compute keyness statistics automatically given pre-divided data.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: With improved diachronic corpus APIs, this will be fully automated within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task is arithmetically closed once corpora are partitioned by period, giving near-complete theoretical replaceability.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Baker (2011) and similar studies specify the period division and keyness measure used, enabling automated parsing.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Corpus sizes and date boundaries are typically reported, allowing AI to reproduce the exact comparison.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Keyness computations are deterministic given the corpus partition and chosen statistic.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Corpus tools like CQPweb and quanteda support time-slice frequency extraction and keyness computation natively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some interpretive effort is needed to contextualise which keywords reflect genuine diachronic shifts versus noise.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Keyword lists from both periods can be numerically matched against published tables to verify replication.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematic variation of the time-slice boundary or keyness threshold maps sensitivity of diachronic findings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires comparing two ranked lists against publication output, adding only modest human audit time.

task: MI-based collocation with fixed window (±3 span, explicit frequency threshold)
notable human research: Church, K. W., & Hanks, P. (1990). Word association norms, mutual information, and lexicography. Computational Linguistics, 16(1), 22–29.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: MI-based collocation with a fixed window is a standard corpus task achievable by researchers using AntConc or SketchEngine.
Research Significance — Explanation: MI collocations are foundational to lexicographic and corpus semantic research and widely cited as a baseline measure.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute MI scores for all word pairs in a window with no human input once parameters are set.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full pipeline automation is well within reach in the next decade, likely already achievable with minor scripting.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: MI collocation is mathematically defined and fully specifiable, making theoretical maximum replaceability complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Church & Hanks (1990) and tool documentation specify the MI formula, window size, and frequency threshold explicitly.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Span, frequency floor, and MI formula variant are reported in virtually every paper using this method.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: MI scores are deterministic floating-point functions of observed and expected co-occurrence counts.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Every major corpus platform (AntConc, SketchEngine, Sketch) and Python NLP library supports MI collocation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is required; rankings emerge entirely from arithmetic frequency ratios.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication is verifiable by exact numerical match of collocate lists and MI scores against published tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Window size, frequency threshold, and span asymmetry can be varied systematically to map MI sensitivity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification reduces to automated numerical comparison, requiring minimal human audit time.

task: Log-Dice collocation ranking (±5 span)
notable human research: Rychlý, P. (2008). A lexicographer-friendly association score. In Proceedings of RASLAN 2008 (pp. 6–9). Masaryk University.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Log-Dice collocations are routinely extracted by corpus linguists using SketchEngine, which implements the measure natively.
Research Significance — Explanation: Log-Dice is a standard lexicographic association measure used across major corpus and dictionary projects.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Log-Dice scores end-to-end given a tokenised corpus and window definition.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already essentially achievable and will be standard pipeline functionality within ten years.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Log-Dice is a closed-form formula making theoretical maximum replaceability complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The Log-Dice formula and ±5 window are explicitly defined in Rychlý (2008) and SketchEngine documentation.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Span and minimum frequency parameters are consistently reported in publications using this measure.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Log-Dice produces identical scores for identical co-occurrence counts with no stochastic element.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: SketchEngine implements Log-Dice natively; Python implementations are readily available in corpus toolkits.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is needed; the measure is a purely computational ranking criterion.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Collocate lists and Log-Dice scores can be numerically matched against published SketchEngine output.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying the window span or frequency threshold provides direct sensitivity mapping of collocate rankings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated list comparison against published word sketches provides near-zero-cost verification.

task: Asymmetric window collocation (e.g., L3R1 non-symmetric span)
notable human research: Brezina, V., McEnery, T., & Wattam, S. (2015). Collocations in context: A new perspective on collocation networks. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 20(2), 139–173.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Asymmetric-window collocation is a minor variant of standard collocation and is feasible for experienced corpus linguists.
Research Significance — Explanation: Asymmetric windows test directional co-occurrence hypotheses, adding methodological refinement to collocation research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can implement any window asymmetry given the numeric L and R parameters, making it fully automatable.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead improvements will trivialise arbitrary window configurations as parameters in standard APIs.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Given explicit L and R window values, theoretical maximum replaceability is complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Brezina et al. (2015) and tool documentation describe asymmetric window parameters with sufficient precision.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: L and R span values are explicitly stated in papers using this method, allowing exact replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Asymmetric co-occurrence counts are deterministic given window specification and tokenised corpus.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Major corpus platforms support custom L/R window spans; Python scripts can implement any asymmetry trivially.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is required once the directional hypothesis and window are defined.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication success is verifiable by numerical match of asymmetric collocate lists against published output.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematic grid search over L and R spans quantifies how directional bias affects collocate rankings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated comparison of ranked collocate lists against published tables keeps verification cost minimal.

task: Collocational profile comparison across registers
notable human research: Xiao, R., & McEnery, T. (2006). Collocation, semantic prosody, and near synonymy: A cross-linguistic perspective. Applied Linguistics, 27(1), 103–129.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Comparing collocational profiles across registers requires corpus access and tool proficiency, making it moderately demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Cross-register collocation comparison reveals register-specific lexical patterns central to corpus-based lexicography.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute register-specific MI or Log-Dice profiles and compare them automatically, with partial interpretive gaps.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved register-classification and comparison tools will push automation close to full within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum falls slightly short of 10 because discourse-level interpretation of cross-register differences may require human expertise.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Xiao & McEnery (2006) describe the association measure, window, and register split with reasonable precision.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Association measure parameters and corpus divisions are reported, though register membership criteria can be implicit.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Statistical profiles are deterministic given identical corpus partitions and association measure.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: SketchEngine and CQPweb support multi-corpus and multi-register collocation comparison natively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some interpretive effort is needed to explain why cross-register differences in collocate rank are linguistically meaningful.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Numerical comparison of collocate rankings across registers against published tables enables objective verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically swapping register boundaries or association measures maps the sensitivity of cross-register contrasts.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification involves comparing two ranked lists per register against published data, adding modest audit effort.

task: Second-order collocation (collocation of collocates)
notable human research: Williams, G. (1998). Collocational networks: Interlocking patterns of lexis in a corpus of plant biology research articles. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 3(1), 151–171.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Second-order collocation requires iterative querying and is more demanding but still feasible for skilled corpus linguists.
Research Significance — Explanation: Second-order collocation reveals semantic networks that go beyond direct co-occurrence, offering deeper lexical insight.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can extract first-order collocates and then their collocates automatically, though semantic coherence checking lags.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Within ten years, network-based collocation tools will automate second-order extraction reliably.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because interpreting second-order networks carries inherent ambiguity.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Williams (1998) outlines the procedure for second-order collocation extraction, though network interpretation is less formalised.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: First-order parameters are explicit, but criteria for filtering second-order collocates are sometimes underspecified.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Second-order frequency counts are deterministic, but network visualisation and filtering choices introduce minor variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Python igraph or NetworkX combined with corpus frequency tools can implement second-order collocation pipelines.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Interpreting which second-order links are linguistically meaningful requires moderate expert judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication of second-order networks can be partially verified by comparing shared nodes and edge weights.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Threshold variation for first- and second-order inclusion maps how network density changes under different criteria.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Validating network topology against published figures requires moderate human review of edge membership.

task: DP-based dispersion analysis (Gries's Deviation of Proportions)
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2008). Dispersions and adjusted frequencies in corpora. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 403–437.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: DP-based dispersion analysis is straightforward for a researcher familiar with Gries's formula and a spreadsheet or R.
Research Significance — Explanation: Gries's DP addresses the frequency-versus-dispersion conflation and is increasingly considered a methodological best practice.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute DP for all items in a frequency list fully automatically given corpus part boundaries.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: DP computation will be a trivial built-in function in all corpus platforms within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: DP is a mathematically closed-form measure with a fully specifiable algorithm, so theoretical maximum replaceability is complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Gries (2008) provides the exact DP formula and corpus-partitioning scheme with full reproducibility.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Corpus part sizes and word frequency per part are typically reported or inferable from published corpus metadata.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: DP scores are deterministic sums of absolute deviations given corpus part divisions and frequencies.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R packages like corpora and Python corpus utilities can compute DP scores from frequency-per-part matrices.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is needed; DP is a pure frequency-distribution statistic.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication is verifiable by exact numerical match of DP values against published per-word dispersion tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying the number and size of corpus parts provides systematic sensitivity analysis of DP estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated numerical comparison against published DP lists keeps verification cost negligible.

task: Juilland's D comparison across registers
notable human research: Juilland, A., & Chang-Rodriguez, E. (1964). Frequency dictionary of Spanish words. Mouton.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Juilland's D across registers is a standard dispersion measure calculable by researchers with basic statistical tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Juilland's D is a classic measure informing lexical stability and is relevant for register-comparative vocabulary studies.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Juilland's D per register and compare values automatically given frequency tables.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is trivial to achieve within ten years as dispersion measures become standard corpus-tool defaults.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Juilland's D is a closed-form formula, making theoretical maximum replaceability complete.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The Juilland formula is well documented, and register-division criteria are typically described in published studies.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Register division and corpus part sizes are usually reported explicitly, enabling exact replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Juilland's D is a deterministic calculation yielding identical results for identical frequency-per-register inputs.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R and Python corpus packages implement Juilland's D; spreadsheet computation is also feasible.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is required; the measure is purely distributional.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: D values per word can be numerically matched against published frequency dictionary entries.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematic variation of register definitions or part sizes quantifies sensitivity of D estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification is automated comparison of D scores against published tables, costing minimal human time.

task: Frequency vs. dispersion trade-off mapping
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2020). Analyzing dispersion. In M. Paquot & S. Th. Gries (Eds.), A practical handbook of corpus linguistics. Springer.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Mapping frequency vs. dispersion trade-offs requires generating scatter plots and is achievable with standard tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: This exploratory mapping reveals the analytic consequences of favouring frequency over dispersion, informing methodological debates.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute both metrics and generate comparison plots automatically, covering most of the task.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full pipeline automation including interpretive commentary will be reachable within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is very high as the computational steps are fully specifiable, though narrative interpretation adds minor residual.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Gries (2020) describes the mapping procedure with sufficient algorithmic detail for automated parsing.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Frequency and corpus-part distributions needed for both metrics are typically available in published corpus data.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Frequency and DP calculations are deterministic; scatter-plot generation is also deterministic given the same data.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R and Python libraries handle both metric computation and visualisation, providing end-to-end toolchain coverage.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minor interpretive effort is needed to contextualise clusters and outliers in the frequency-dispersion space.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Scatter-plot patterns and computed metric pairs can be compared numerically or visually against published figures.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematic threshold variation reveals how different frequency floors change the trade-off mapping.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires visual or numerical comparison of the scatter plot against published versions, adding modest audit cost.

task: CQL/CQP concordance search with explicit query string
notable human research: Evert, S., & Hardie, A. (2011). Twenty-first century Corpus Workbench: Updating a query architecture for the new millennium. In Proceedings of the Corpus Linguistics 2011 Conference. University of Birmingham.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: CQL/CQP concordance search is a routine task for corpus linguists; the query syntax is learnable within hours.
Research Significance — Explanation: Exact concordance search is the bedrock operation for virtually all corpus-based pattern analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can translate natural-language descriptions into CQL queries and execute them automatically.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation of query generation and execution will be standard in next-generation corpus platforms.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Given an explicit query string, concordance retrieval is entirely deterministic and fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: CQL syntax is formally specified in CQPweb documentation; extraction parameters are stated in the query string itself.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: The query string and corpus are by definition fully explicit, making parameter reporting trivially complete.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Identical CQL queries on identical corpora produce bit-identical concordance output.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CQPweb, SketchEngine, and corpus Python wrappers all execute CQL queries natively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is needed for the retrieval step; interpretation of lines is a separate downstream task.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Concordance output can be exactly matched against published KWIC lines or hit counts for verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematic query variation (e.g., expanding/contracting POS constraints) directly maps retrieval sensitivity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification is trivial: hit counts and sample lines are directly comparable against published concordance output.

task: Lexico-grammatical pattern extraction via dependency parsing (SVO triples)
notable human research: Mason, O., & Hunston, S. (2004). The automatic recognition of verb patterns: A feasibility study. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 9(2), 253–270.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Dependency-parsed SVO extraction requires running an NLP parser and filtering triples, which is moderately demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: SVO pattern extraction enables automated grammar-pattern analysis at scale, linking corpus and constructional approaches.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI parsers (spaCy, Stanford NLP) extract SVO triples automatically with high but not perfect accuracy.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Parser accuracy improvements over ten years will make SVO extraction near-perfect for standard English.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: SVO extraction is fully specifiable given a deterministic parser, so theoretical maximum replaceability is very high.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Mason & Hunston (2004) describe the pattern-matching procedure but parser-specific implementation details vary.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Dependency relation labels and filtering rules are sometimes underspecified, requiring reasonable implementation choices.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Parser output is deterministic for a fixed model version; different parser versions introduce minor cross-version variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: spaCy, StanfordNLP, and UDPipe provide mature dependency parsers with SVO extraction examples available.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement is needed once extraction rules are fixed; error analysis adds minor overhead.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Extracted SVO triples can be compared against published pattern lists or frequency tables for verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying parser thresholds or relation-type filters provides systematic sensitivity mapping of extraction coverage.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires comparing extracted triple frequencies against published counts, adding modest human review.

task: Constructional frequency profiling (syntactic construction counts across genres)
notable human research: Stefanowitsch, A. (2013). Collostructional analysis. In T. Hoffmann & G. Trousdale (Eds.), The Oxford handbook of construction grammar. Oxford University Press.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Syntactic construction counting across genres requires parser output and genre-partitioned corpora, demanding moderate expertise.
Research Significance — Explanation: Construction frequency profiling tests usage-based predictions and is central to corpus-based construction grammar.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can annotate and count syntactic constructions per genre automatically, though construction-definition precision varies.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved construction parsers tied to constructicon databases will automate this task reliably within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task approaches full theoretical replaceability once construction definitions are formalised in machine-readable form.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Stefanowitsch (2013) describes collostructional counting procedures with sufficient detail, though construction boundaries vary.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Construction definitions are often described qualitatively, requiring operationalisation choices that affect replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Construction counts are deterministic given a fixed parser and construction definition; definitional variation introduces noise.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: spaCy and NLTK parsers combined with construction-detection scripts can execute frequency profiling end-to-end.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate judgement is needed to define construction boundaries and interpret inter-genre frequency contrasts.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Replication is verifiable by comparing construction frequency tables against published genre profiles.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying construction definitions or parser settings maps sensitivity of frequency profiles across genres.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human review of construction-detection rules and boundary cases moderately increases verification cost.

task: Semantic prosody assessment via concordance sampling
notable human research: Louw, B. (1993). Irony in the text or insincerity in the writer? The diagnostic potential of semantic prosodies. In M. Baker, G. Francis, & E. Tognini-Bonelli (Eds.), Text and technology (pp. 157–176). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Semantic prosody assessment requires reading many concordance lines and making holistic evaluative judgements, which is demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Semantic prosody is a foundational concept in corpus semantics; identifying it rigorously distinguishes expert corpus analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can flag candidate prosodies from sentiment signals but cannot reliably match expert holistic concordance judgement.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Neural LLMs will substantially improve prosody detection within a decade but may still miss subtle evaluative nuance.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Full prosody assessment may always retain a residual human element due to the subjective nature of holistic evaluation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Louw (1993) describes the qualitative sampling procedure but does not formalise decision criteria for prosody assignment.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: The concordance sampling procedure is described but the threshold for labelling a prosody is rarely made explicit.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Different analysts sampling different concordance lines can reach different prosody assessments, making outputs non-deterministic.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Concordance tools provide the data, but prosody judgement itself lacks a mature automated classification pipeline.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: High interpretive load: the analyst must synthesise many contextual cues into a global evaluative judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published prosody labels can be compared against AI assignments, but inter-annotator disagreement limits ground-truth certainty.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying the concordance sample size or concordance query alters prosody assignments, but sensitivity is hard to quantify.
Verification Cost — Explanation: A human expert must re-read sampled concordance lines to verify AI prosody labels, incurring substantial audit cost.

task: Discourse preference profiling (co-occurrence of evaluative lexis around a target)
notable human research: Partington, A., Duguid, A., & Taylor, C. (2013). Patterns and meanings in discourse: Theory and practice in corpus-assisted discourse studies (CADS). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Discourse preference profiling requires qualitative corpus reading alongside frequency counts, making it cognitively demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Identifying evaluative lexis co-occurrence patterns is central to CADS methodology and informs critical discourse research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can extract co-occurrence frequencies around target words but struggles with evaluative interpretation of patterns.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead LLMs with improved discourse understanding will push automation closer to full for frequency-level steps.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is limited by the inherent subjectivity of 'evaluative' lexis identification across discourse contexts.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Partington et al. (2013) outline the CADS procedure conceptually, but operationalisation choices vary substantially across studies.

notable human research: Tiedemann, J. (2011). Bitext alignment. Morgan & Claypool.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Parallel corpus sentence alignment and translation-equivalent extraction require alignment tools and bilingual corpora, moderately demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Extracting translation equivalents at scale is foundational to translation studies corpora and multilingual NLP resource creation.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI alignment tools (Hunalign, GIZA++) automate sentence alignment and equivalent extraction with high accuracy.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already largely achieved; next-decade neural aligners will be near-perfect.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is very high; some residual error in non-compositional translation equivalents limits perfect replaceability.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Tiedemann (2011) fully specifies sentence-alignment algorithms with reproducible parameter descriptions.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Alignment parameters (e.g., Hunalign thresholds) and corpus metadata are reported in most parallel corpus papers.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Alignment outputs are deterministic for a fixed aligner version and corpus; different aligner versions introduce minor variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Hunalign, GIZA++, Moses, and Opus provide mature end-to-end alignment and equivalent extraction pipelines.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement needed; alignment quality is assessed computationally with precision/recall metrics.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Alignment accuracy can be objectively evaluated against gold-standard human-aligned test sets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying aligner thresholds or algorithm choice maps sensitivity of alignment coverage and precision.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification against gold-standard alignments is automated, requiring minimal human review.

task: Translation universals testing (simplification, explicitation, normalisation)
notable human research: Baker, M. (1993). Corpus linguistics and translation studies: Implications and applications. In M. Baker, G. Francis, & E. Tognini-Bonelli (Eds.), Text and technology (pp. 233–250). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Testing translation universals requires building a translated corpus, computing multiple metrics, and interpreting trends, which is demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Translation universals research tests fundamental hypotheses about translated language, making it theoretically significant.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute type-token ratios, sentence-length profiles, and explicitation metrics automatically, but interpretation lags.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved translation-corpus pipelines will automate the computational steps fully within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because characterising which patterns constitute genuine universals retains interpretive residual.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Baker (1993) outlines the translation universals hypotheses but does not specify a single agreed operationalisation.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Metric operationalisations (e.g., which connectives count as explicitation) are often researcher-defined rather than standardised.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Simplification and explicitation metrics vary in definition across studies, introducing non-determinism at the operationalisation level.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Corpus frequency tools, TTR scripts, and cohesion-marker lists provide partial toolchain coverage.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive judgement is needed to decide which empirical patterns constitute evidence for a universal.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Individual metrics can be numerically verified; overall universal-status claims are harder to evaluate objectively.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying metric definitions maps sensitivity of 'universal' detection to operationalisation choices.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human expert review of metric choices and interpretive conclusions adds moderate verification overhead.

task: TTR and MTLD comparison across text types
notable human research: McCarthy, P. M., & Jarvis, S. (2010). MTLD, vocd-D, and HD-D: A validation study of sophisticated approaches to lexical diversity assessment. Behavior Research Methods, 42(2), 381–392.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: TTR and MTLD computation are simple frequency operations executable with any text-analysis library or even a spreadsheet.
Research Significance — Explanation: TTR and MTLD are standard lexical diversity benchmarks widely used in applied linguistics and language assessment.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute TTR and MTLD across text types fully automatically from raw text files.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already achieved and will remain trivially automatable within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Both metrics are closed-form algorithms fully specifiable from raw text, giving theoretical maximum replaceability of 10.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: McCarthy & Jarvis (2010) fully specify the MTLD and vocd algorithms with reproducible parameters.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Text type definitions and metric parameters (e.g., MTLD factor threshold) are always reported in published studies.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: TTR and MTLD computations are deterministic given fixed tokenisation and text boundaries.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Python textstat, lexicalrichness, and R koRpus packages implement TTR and MTLD natively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is needed; the metrics are purely computational.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Metric values can be exactly compared against published per-text or per-group tables for verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying text length or MTLD factor threshold maps sensitivity of lexical diversity estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated numerical comparison against published tables keeps verification cost negligible.

task: Automatic term extraction (C-value / NC-value)
notable human research: Frantzi, K., Ananiadou, S., & Mima, H. (2000). Automatic recognition of multi-word terms: The C-value/NC-value method. International Journal on Digital Libraries, 3(2), 115–130.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: C-value/NC-value term extraction requires a POS tagger and frequency counter, which is feasible with standard NLP tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Automatic term extraction is important for terminology research, ontology building, and specialised corpus lexicography.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run C-value/NC-value pipelines fully automatically from domain corpora using existing implementations.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already largely achievable; next-decade improvements in domain adaptation will perfect it.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The C-value algorithm is fully specified, giving very high theoretical maximum replaceability with minor residual for term validation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Frantzi et al. (2000) specify the C-value formula and NC-value context-weight procedure reproducibly.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Frequency threshold, POS filter, and context-weight parameters are reported in the original paper.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: C-value and NC-value computations are deterministic given a fixed POS tagger and frequency threshold.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Java and Python implementations of C-value are available; TermSuite and other term extraction tools implement it.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement needed for extraction; domain expert validation of final term candidates adds minor load.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Extracted term lists can be compared against published C-value ranked lists from the same corpus.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying frequency thresholds and POS filters maps sensitivity of extracted term inventories.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human domain-expert review of borderline term candidates adds minor verification overhead.

task: MIPVU metaphor identification on corpus data
notable human research: Steen, G. J., Dorst, A. G., Herrmann, J. B., Kaal, A. A., Krennmayr, T., & Pasma, T. (2010). A method for linguistic metaphor identification: From MIP to MIPVU. John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: MIPVU metaphor identification requires trained annotators applying a detailed four-step procedure to every word, which is very labour-intensive.
Research Significance — Explanation: MIPVU provides the most rigorous and widely adopted procedure for corpus-based metaphor annotation, central to metaphor research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can flag potential metaphors using sense-disambiguation heuristics, but MIPVU step-by-step decision making is largely unreplicated.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: LLM-assisted metaphor annotation will improve substantially in the next decade but may not fully replicate the four-step MIPVU procedure.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is constrained by the inherent subjectivity of the 'more basic meaning' criterion in the MIPVU decision tree.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Steen et al. (2010) specify the MIPVU procedure in detail, but applying the four steps requires contextual semantic judgement.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: The MIPVU decision steps are published, but many intermediate criteria rely on world knowledge not fully specifiable computationally.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Different annotators applying MIPVU to the same text typically achieve inter-rater agreement well below 100%, indicating non-determinism.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: No mature automated MIPVU pipeline exists; partial tools use WSD or metaphor lexicons but do not follow MIPVU exactly.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Very high interpretive load: every content word requires a contextual basic-meaning comparison against dictionary senses.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Annotation output can be compared against published MIPVU gold-standard corpora to estimate agreement.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying the 'basic meaning' resource (WordNet vs. MRC) maps sensitivity of metaphor-rate estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Expert re-annotation of a sample to verify AI MIPVU labels is time-intensive, incurring high verification cost.

task: LDA topic modelling for corpus exploration
notable human research: Brookes, G., & McEnery, T. (2019). The utility of topic modelling for discourse studies: A critical evaluation. Discourse Studies, 21(1), 3–21.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: LDA topic modelling requires setting model parameters and interpreting output topics, demanding both technical and linguistic expertise.
Research Significance — Explanation: LDA is a widely used exploratory tool for large corpus summarisation, bridging computational and discourse-analytical corpus work.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can train LDA models and extract topic-word distributions automatically, though topic labelling remains AI-challenging.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead neural topic models (BERTopic, CTM) will automate topic modelling and labelling more reliably.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because meaningful topic labelling retains an irreducible human interpretive step.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Brookes & McEnery (2019) describe LDA application to discourse corpora, but number-of-topics selection is corpus-dependent.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Number of topics (K), alpha/beta hyperparameters, and stopword lists are often but not always fully reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: LDA output varies across random seeds; different K values produce different (valid) topic solutions, reducing determinism.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Gensim, scikit-learn, and Mallet provide mature LDA implementations covering end-to-end topic modelling.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive effort is needed to label extracted topics and assess their discourse significance.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Topic coherence scores (e.g., C_v) provide partial objective evaluation; full discourse interpretation requires expert review.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying K and hyperparameters produces topic sensitivity profiles over the corpus.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human topic labelling and coherence review add moderate verification overhead beyond automated coherence metrics.

task: Effect-size-based keyness meta-analysis (Log Ratio / %DIFF)
notable human research: Hardie, A. (2014). Log Ratio: An informal introduction [Blog post]. ESRC Centre for Corpus Approaches to Social Science (CASS).
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Effect-size-based keyness meta-analysis (Log Ratio / %DIFF) is straightforward for researchers familiar with basic statistics.
Research Significance — Explanation: Effect-size keyness measures address statistical significance inflation in large corpora, advancing methodological rigour in corpus research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Log Ratio and %DIFF for all vocabulary items fully automatically from frequency tables.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already achievable and will remain trivially automatable within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: These are closed-form formulas applied to frequency tables, giving theoretical maximum replaceability of 10.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Hardie (2014) defines Log Ratio precisely and %DIFF is fully specified in Gabrielatos & Marchi (2012).
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Corpus frequencies and sizes needed for these measures are always reported in keyness studies.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Log Ratio and %DIFF are deterministic floating-point functions of frequency counts.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Python pandas and R base functions compute these measures in a single line from a frequency data frame.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: No interpretive judgement is needed for the numeric computation step.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Ranked lists can be exactly compared against published keyness tables for verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying frequency floors maps how effect-size keyness rankings change under different minimum-frequency thresholds.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated comparison of effect-size-ranked keyword lists against published output keeps verification cost negligible.

task: Register-feature co-occurrence network analysis
notable human research: Brezina, V., McEnery, T., & Wattam, S. (2015). Collocations in context: A new perspective on collocation networks. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 20(2), 139–173.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Register-feature co-occurrence network construction requires POS-tagged corpora and graph-construction tools, which are moderately demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Co-occurrence networks of register features reveal structural dependencies in register variation, advancing register theory.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute feature co-occurrences, build adjacency matrices, and generate network graphs automatically.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved register-feature extraction combined with graph-analytics APIs will automate this fully within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because network community labelling and register interpretation retain interpretive residual.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Brezina et al. (2015) describe network construction for collocations; adaptation to register features requires methodological extension.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Feature extraction parameters and edge-weight thresholds are sometimes underspecified for register network tasks.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Co-occurrence matrix construction is deterministic; community-detection algorithms introduce minor run-to-run variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: NetworkX, igraph, and the R qgraph package provide end-to-end toolchain coverage for network construction and analysis.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive effort is needed to label network communities and connect them to register-theoretical constructs.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Network topology statistics (modularity, degree distribution) can be compared against published network summaries.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying edge-weight thresholds or community-detection algorithms maps sensitivity of network structure to parameter choices.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human review of community labels and topology interpretation adds moderate verification overhead.

task: Collexeme analysis (construction–lexeme attraction)
notable human research: Stefanowitsch, A., & Gries, S. Th. (2003). Collostructions: Investigating the interaction of words and constructions. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 8(2), 209–243.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Collexeme analysis requires frequency extraction, Fisher's exact test computation, and effect-size ranking, which is feasible with R or Python.
Research Significance — Explanation: Collexeme analysis is the dominant quantitative method for testing construction–lexeme attraction in corpus-based construction grammar.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run collexeme analysis pipelines fully automatically using existing R/Python implementations.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already essentially achievable and will be standard practice within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task is a fully specified statistical test on corpus frequency tables, giving theoretical maximum replaceability of 10.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Stefanowitsch & Gries (2003) fully specify the Fisher's exact test procedure and association-strength ranking.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Construction frequency, lexeme frequency, and corpus size are always reported in collexeme studies.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fisher's exact test is deterministic given frequency inputs; collostruction strength rankings follow uniquely.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: The collostructions R package and Python implementations execute the full collexeme analysis pipeline.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement needed for the statistical computation; construction-meaning interpretation is a downstream step.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Collostruction strength rankings can be exactly compared against published collexeme tables from the same corpus.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying corpus size or construction definition maps sensitivity of collexeme rankings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated numerical comparison of collostruction strength tables against published results keeps verification cost minimal.

task: Distinctive collexeme analysis (near-synonymous construction comparison)
notable human research: Gries, S. Th., & Stefanowitsch, A. (2004). Extending collostructional analysis: A corpus-based perspective on 'alternations'. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 9(1), 97–129.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Distinctive collexeme analysis extends collexeme analysis by comparing two constructions, requiring the same R/Python tooling.
Research Significance — Explanation: Distinctive collexeme analysis directly quantifies near-synonymous construction distinctions, central to corpus-based cognitive grammar.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can execute the full distinctive collexeme pipeline automatically using the collostructions R package.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already essentially achievable and will remain standard within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The task is a fully specified comparative statistical test, giving theoretical maximum replaceability of 10.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Gries & Stefanowitsch (2004) fully specify the comparative test procedure with reproducible frequency inputs.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Frequency counts for both constructions and shared lexemes are always reported in published studies.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: The comparative Fisher's test is deterministic given the two-by-two frequency tables for each lexeme.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: The collostructions R package implements distinctive collexeme analysis natively with a single function call.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement needed for the statistical step; construction-meaning contrast interpretation is downstream.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Collostruction strength rankings for both constructions can be exactly compared against published tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying construction membership definitions maps sensitivity of distinctive collexeme rankings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated comparison of comparative strength tables against published output keeps verification cost minimal.

task: Behavioural profile analysis (polysemy clustering via ID tags)
notable human research: Gries, S. Th., & Divjak, D. (2009). Behavioral profiles: A corpus-based approach to cognitive semantic analysis. In V. Evans & S. Pourcel (Eds.), New directions in cognitive linguistics (pp. 57–75). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Behavioural profile analysis requires multidimensional feature coding and clustering, demanding statistical and linguistic expertise.
Research Significance — Explanation: Behavioural profiles provide a multidimensional operationalisation of polysemy, advancing corpus-based cognitive semantics.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can extract ID tag vectors and apply clustering algorithms semi-automatically, though feature coding quality varies.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved feature extraction and clustering will push automation to near-full within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because deciding which features define behaviourally distinct senses retains interpretive residual.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Gries & Divjak (2009) specify the feature types and clustering procedure with sufficient detail for automated parsing.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Feature types are published, but which ID tags to include and how to bin them are often corpus-specific choices.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Clustering results vary with algorithm and seed; feature extraction is deterministic given fixed annotation guidelines.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R cluster packages and Python scikit-learn provide clustering algorithms; ID tag extraction requires corpus-specific scripting.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive effort is needed to label clusters as distinct senses and validate against dictionary polysemy.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Cluster membership can be compared against published sense profiles; cluster number validation requires expert judgement.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying clustering algorithm, number of clusters, or feature set maps sensitivity of sense-grouping solutions.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human semantic review of cluster assignments adds moderate verification overhead.

task: Idiolectal style marker profiling
notable human research: Grant, T. (2013). TXT 4N6: Method, consistency, and distinctiveness in the analysis of SMS text messages. Journal of Law and Policy, 21(2), 467–494.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Idiolectal style marker profiling requires building most-frequent-word or n-gram profiles and comparing them, which is technically feasible.
Research Significance — Explanation: Forensic stylometry underpins legal authorship attribution and is thus practically significant beyond academic research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can extract idiolectal features and compute pairwise style distances automatically, but forensic-grade reliability is uncertain.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead stylometric models will substantially improve forensic attribution accuracy.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because forensic conclusions require human-expert testimony in legal contexts.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Grant (2013) describes the feature extraction and comparison procedure, though feature selection varies across forensic cases.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Feature types (character n-grams, function words) are described, but case-specific feature selection is analyst-dependent.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Feature extraction is deterministic; pairwise distance computation is deterministic given fixed features and metric.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: The stylo R package and JGAAP forensic tool provide adequate toolchain coverage for idiolectal profiling.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive judgement is needed to assess whether style distance exceeds a meaningful attribution threshold.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Attribution results can be benchmarked against ground-truth authorship in test datasets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying feature set size or distance metric maps sensitivity of attribution decisions.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human forensic-expert review of attribution conclusions adds moderate to high verification overhead in legal contexts.

task: Speech-act frequency profiling in spoken corpora
notable human research: Aijmer, K., & Rühlemann, C. (Eds.). (2015). Corpus pragmatics: A handbook. Cambridge University Press.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Speech-act frequency profiling requires either manual annotation or applying a trained speech-act classifier to spoken corpora.
Research Significance — Explanation: Speech-act profiling quantifies pragmatic functions at scale, supporting interactional and genre-based pragmatics research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can apply trained speech-act classifiers automatically, but classification accuracy for fine-grained acts remains limited.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead conversational AI and pragmatic classifiers will substantially improve speech-act classification.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because fine-grained speech-act boundary ambiguity retains irreducible annotation subjectivity.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Aijmer & Rühlemann (2015) describe speech-act annotation approaches, but no single universal annotation scheme is fully specified.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Speech-act categories and frequency normalisation are described qualitatively; exact annotation criteria vary across schemes.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Speech-act annotation is non-deterministic for ambiguous utterances, producing inter-annotator disagreement above chance.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Trained speech-act classifiers exist for some tagsets; end-to-end pipelines for novel corpora require fine-tuning.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: High interpretive load: speech-act boundaries and category assignments require contextual pragmatic reasoning.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Annotated corpora with gold-standard speech-act labels provide partial ground truth for automatic classifier evaluation.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying the tagset granularity or classifier confidence threshold maps sensitivity of speech-act frequency profiles.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human expert re-annotation of ambiguous utterances to validate classifier output incurs relatively high verification cost.

task: Variationist frequency analysis with mixed-effects modelling
notable human research: Tagliamonte, S. A., & Baayen, R. H. (2012). Models, forests, and trees of York English: Was/were variation as a case study for statistical practice. Language Variation and Change, 24(2), 135–178.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Variationist analysis with mixed-effects models requires R expertise and careful random-effect structure specification, which is demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Mixed-effects modelling for variationist data is increasingly the methodological gold standard in corpus-based sociolinguistics.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can fit lme4 mixed-effects models given a structured frequency data frame, though model-selection guidance is partial.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Decade-ahead AutoML for mixed-effects models will make this largely automated, though model interpretation will require expertise.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because random-effect structure selection and result interpretation retain expert judgement residual.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Tagliamonte & Baayen (2012) describe the modelling workflow and report all fixed and random effects explicitly.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Predictor variables, random-effect groupings, and corpus metadata are typically reported, enabling model replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Mixed-effects model fitting is deterministic given the data and formula; only REML optimisation introduces minor numerical variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R lme4 and Python statsmodels provide mature mixed-effects modelling toolchains.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate interpretive effort is needed to select the optimal random-effect structure and interpret fixed-effect estimates.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Model coefficients and random-effect variances can be compared against published tables for numerical verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying fixed-effect predictors or random-effect groupings produces sensitivity profiles for model estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human expert review of model specification and interpretation adds moderate verification overhead.

task: Spelling-variant normalisation pipeline (VARD)
notable human research: Baron, A., & Rayson, P. (2008). VARD2: A tool for dealing with spelling variation in historical corpora. In Proceedings of the Postgraduate Conference in Corpus Linguistics. Aston University.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Running VARD2 spelling normalisation requires configuring the tool and reviewing semi-automatic suggestions, which is feasible but time-consuming.
Research Significance — Explanation: Spelling normalisation is a prerequisite for historical corpus analysis, directly affecting all downstream frequency and collocation results.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run VARD2 or neural normalisation models largely automatically, though accuracy on rare variants is imperfect.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Neural spelling normalisation will become highly accurate within a decade for most historical English varieties.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because novel historical spelling variants not in training data may always require human decisions.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Baron & Rayson (2008) describe the VARD2 pipeline and configuration with reproducible parameter descriptions.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: VARD2 configuration files and training corpora are documented, allowing reproducible normalisation pipelines.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: VARD2 applies deterministic substitution rules; the semi-interactive mode introduces minor decision variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: VARD2 is a mature, freely available tool specifically designed for historical English spelling normalisation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minor interpretive judgement is needed for borderline variant decisions and domain-specific archaic forms.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Normalisation accuracy can be evaluated against gold-standard manually normalised test corpora.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying VARD2 confidence thresholds and candidate-list size maps sensitivity of normalisation coverage.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human spot-checking of VARD2 suggestions for rare variants adds moderate verification overhead.

task: Forced-alignment-based duration analysis (Montreal Forced Aligner)
notable human research: McAuliffe, M., Socolof, M., Mihuc, S., Wagner, M., & Sonderegger, M. (2017). Montreal Forced Aligner: Trainable text-speech alignment using Kaldi. In Proceedings of Interspeech 2017 (pp. 498–502).
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Running the Montreal Forced Aligner requires CLI expertise, audio files, and transcripts, making it moderately demanding.
Research Significance — Explanation: Forced-alignment-based duration analysis enables large-scale phonetic corpus research previously limited by manual segmentation.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI (MFA) can align and extract duration statistics automatically from audio/transcript pairs with high accuracy.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: MFA and successor tools will achieve near-perfect alignment quality within a decade across diverse languages and varieties.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because alignment errors on reduced speech and non-native accents require manual correction.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: McAuliffe et al. (2017) fully specify the MFA pipeline, acoustic model, and dictionary parameters.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Acoustic model, pronunciation dictionary, and sample rate are reported in MFA studies, enabling exact replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: MFA alignment is deterministic for fixed acoustic model and dictionary; model version may introduce minor cross-version variability.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: MFA is a mature, actively maintained tool with pretrained acoustic models for multiple languages.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement needed; alignment quality is assessed objectively with boundary-error metrics.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Alignment accuracy can be evaluated against manually segmented TextGrid gold standards.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying acoustic model, dictionary, or alignment beam maps sensitivity of duration measurements to alignment parameters.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human review of alignment errors in acoustically challenging segments adds moderate verification overhead.

task: MLU and morpheme-emergence profiling on CHILDES data
notable human research: MacWhinney, B. (2000). The CHILDES project: Tools for analyzing talk (3rd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: MLU and morpheme-emergence profiling on CHILDES requires using CLAN software, which is feasible for researchers trained in the system.
Research Significance — Explanation: MLU and morpheme-order analysis are foundational metrics in child language acquisition research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run CLAN MLUM and morpheme-search commands automatically on CHILDES transcripts.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation via CLAN scripting and Python CHILDES parsers will be standard within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Theoretical maximum is slightly below 10 because morpheme identification in noisy child speech transcripts retains minor ambiguity.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: MacWhinney (2000) and CLAN documentation fully specify MLU computation and morpheme-tagging procedures.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Age ranges, utterance counts, and morpheme definitions used in published CHILDES studies are typically fully reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: MLU computation is deterministic given fixed CLAN coding; morpheme tagging follows standard CHAT conventions.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CLAN software and PyLangAcq provide complete toolchain coverage for CHILDES data analysis.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minor interpretive judgement is needed for ambiguous morpheme boundaries in child speech transcripts.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: MLU values and morpheme-emergence ages can be compared against published CHILDES normative tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Systematically varying age range or morpheme definition maps sensitivity of emergence profiles.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Human review of CHAT coding decisions for ambiguous child utterances adds minor verification overhead.

task: CEFR-aligned lexical profiling (EVP / TAALES)
notable human research: Kyle, K., Crossley, S., & Berger, C. (2018). The tool for the automatic analysis of lexical sophistication (TAALES): Version 2.0. Behavior Research Methods, 50(3), 1030–1046.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: CEFR-aligned lexical profiling with TAALES requires running the tool on a text file, which is straightforward for applied linguists.
Research Significance — Explanation: CEFR-aligned profiling is central to language testing, curriculum development, and automated text readability research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run TAALES or EVP profiling fully automatically from raw text files.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Full automation is already essentially achievable and will remain trivially automatable within a decade.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: TAALES and EVP are fully specified word-list lookup tools, giving very high theoretical maximum replaceability.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Kyle et al. (2018) fully document the TAALES indices, word lists, and CEFR band mappings.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: CEFR word lists, text type categories, and normalisation bases are published and consistently reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: TAALES output is deterministic given a fixed version of the tool and word lists.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: TAALES software (Windows/Mac) and PyTAALES provide end-to-end toolchain coverage.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretive judgement is needed; all CEFR band assignments are pre-specified in the word lists.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: TAALES index scores can be exactly compared against published per-text or per-genre profile tables.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Varying CEFR band definitions or word-list versions maps sensitivity of lexical profile estimates.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Automated comparison of TAALES scores against published tables keeps verification cost minimal.
